title: pi-context update --dryRun reports a version-bump schema as the would-act `resynced` set, not its precise migrate/block outcome — the preview can over-report
description: Under --dryRun, updateContext's catalog-ahead arm short-circuits BEFORE resyncSchema (no write) and reports the schema under `resynced` as the would-act set, because the concrete resynced/migrated/blocked outcome is unknowable without running the forward migration. So a version-bump schema that a live run would `migrate` (items forward-migrated) — or `block` (no safe chain, or migrated items fail revalidation) — is previewed as `resynced`. The would-register migration declarations (migrationsRegistered) are computed accurately read-only, but a live blocked run rolls those declarations back, so --dryRun can also list migrationsRegistered a live run would not keep. This undercuts FEAT-006's stated guarantee that --dry-run reports the full set of changes update would make: the per-schema OUTCOME under dryRun is optimistic, not exact, precisely when a schema is at risk of blocking.

evidence.reference: updateContext catalog-ahead dryRun branch pushes name onto result.resynced (the would-act set) and computes migrationsRegistered read-only; the live resynced/migrated/blocked classification and the block-path rollback of migrations.json are not modelled under dryRun

impact: A user previewing an update sees `resynced` for a schema a live run would actually migrate or refuse (block), and migrationsRegistered may list decls a blocked live run would roll back — the dry-run preview can over-state what an update would do, exactly for the schemas most at risk.
proposed_resolution: Under --dryRun compute the precise per-schema version-bump outcome READ-ONLY (find the catalog migration chain, forward-migrate the populated block in memory, AJV-validate against the new schema) and classify resynced vs migrated vs blocked — reporting migrationsRegistered only for non-blocked outcomes — without writing, so the preview matches a live run.
